---
title: "Executive Summary"
author:
    - name: "Sibahle Mabhuda"
    - name: "Yvette Maria Monari"
    - name: "Tayaphon Rodsai" 
    - name: "Felix Sandile Thwala"
    - name: "Pelin Kimiz" 
    - name: "Uyazi Dube"
---
This project examines how personal values shape political attitudes across generations in Europe between 2018 and 2024. Using data from the European Social Survey (Rounds 9–11), the study investigates whether shifts in societal context, particularly the COVID-19 pandemic, have altered the relationship between individual value systems and political orientations. The analysis is grounded in the Schwartz Theory of Basic Human Values, which identifies ten fundamental motivational values that influence attitudes and behavior across cultures.

The project focuses on understanding how core values such as Universalism, Security, and Tradition relate to political identity and policy preferences across generational cohorts. By comparing respondents from Generation Z, Millennials, Generation X, and Baby Boomers, the study explores whether younger generations are developing distinct value structures that may reshape future political dynamics in Europe.

Six research questions guide the analysis. The first establishes the overall predictive framework: to what extent do Schwartz's ten basic human values predict political attitudes in Europe, and which values carry the greatest influence on political self-placement? The second examines cross-national variation, asking whether the strength of these value-attitude relationships differs systematically across European countries. The third turns to generational differences, comparing how political attitudes and value priorities vary across cohorts in both the pre- and post-pandemic landscape.

The fourth question investigates whether age cohorts differ in life satisfaction and institutional trust — probing whether these broader wellbeing indicators follow generational patterns distinct from political attitudes. The fifth evaluates the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic directly, asking whether it produced measurable shifts in Conservation values — Security, Conformity, and Tradition — and whether these shifts are reflected in changing levels of political trust between Round 9 and Round 11. The sixth focuses on Universalism and redistribution, examining whether individuals who prioritize equality and social justice show stronger support for government-led income redistribution, and whether this relationship has changed for Generation Z compared to older cohorts.

Together, these questions combine generational, temporal, and cross-national perspectives to provide a comprehensive picture of how evolving value systems may influence the future trajectory of political attitudes and stability within Europe.
